name: Materials Engineering Minor

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: No requirement groups are parsed (requirementGroups 0), and more seriously the captured course pool does not represent materials engineering well: it is chemistry-dominated (CHEM 125/126/222/243/336/340/360/361/473/474/412/480/491) with only BME 304 Material Fundamentals and CHE 444 touching materials directly, and it is byte-for-byte identical to the Chemistry Minor (both from page 580, same metrics: 83 referenced, 27 missing, depth 8). A materials-engineering minor would be expected to center on structure-property relationships, characterization, and material classes (metals/ceramics/polymers/composites), none of which the pool foregrounds. The extraction appears to have attached a shared department listing to both minors.
dimensions.suggestion: Replace the shared chemistry pool with a genuine materials-engineering course set built around MTEN 201 Introductory Principles of Materials Engineering, BME 304, and courses on structure-property relationships, characterization and specific material classes.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The referenced chains are sensible where they exist (deepestPrereqChain 8 with grade conditions), but prerequisite health is undermined by missingCourseCount 27 and by the fact that the resolved courses are largely chemistry rather than materials. The missingCoursesSample (ACCT 200, BME 300, CE 300, CHE 300, CHEM 300, CPT 300, ECE 200/300, EM 600, ENE 200, ENGR 200/211, ENTR 400, FIN 200) is dominated by non-materials placeholders, so the graph does not describe a materials-minor pathway.
dimensions.suggestion: Rebuild the prerequisite graph around actual materials courses (e.g., MTEN 201 to advanced materials electives) so the 27 dangling non-materials references disappear.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes appear in the data. For a materials-engineering minor this is a real gap because the minor should certify a specific competency in materials selection and structure-property analysis, and no such statement is present.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 3-5 outcomes stating what the materials minor certifies (e.g., ability to relate microstructure to properties, select materials for an application, interpret characterization data).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: There is no credit information: statedTotalCredits, summedTermCredits and creditReconcileDelta are all null, a data-consistency caveat rather than a proven imbalance. As presented, a student cannot see the minor's credit requirement or distribution, and the mismatch between the label (materials) and the chemistry-heavy pool compounds the uncertainty.
dimensions.suggestion: State the total credit requirement and list the specific materials courses that count, so the minor's load and content are transparent and match its title.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The captured page is incomplete and, unusually, mislabeled relative to its content: no plan of study, no credit total, no description, and a chemistry-dominated pool identical to the Chemistry Minor. A reader cannot tell what the materials minor actually requires or how it differs from the chemistry minor.
dimensions.suggestion: Render a distinct materials-minor page with its own description, a materials-centered required/elective list, and a credit total, and fix the parser so it stops sharing the chemistry minor's listing.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: As captured, the pool does not surface the core materials-engineering topics employers expect — crystallography and phase equilibria, mechanical behavior, characterization (microscopy/diffraction), and functional/electronic materials — relying instead on chemistry courses and a single materials-fundamentals course (BME 304). This makes the minor's currency for materials careers weak as presented, notwithstanding the underlying discipline's strong relevance.
dimensions.suggestion: Introduce modern materials content (characterization methods, electronic/energy materials, polymers/composites) so the minor reflects contemporary materials-engineering practice rather than general chemistry.

descriptionQuality.justification: The sampled descriptions are present and specific (26 of 26, none thin), but they describe chemistry courses far more than materials-engineering courses (only BME 304 Material Fundamentals and CHE 444 are clearly materials-oriented); description quality is fine, but the courses described are largely off-topic for the minor.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Individual prerequisites carry grade conditions, but with 27 dangling references — mostly non-materials placeholders (BME 300, CE 300, ECE 300, EM 600, ENTR 400) — and a pool that does not match the minor's title, the actual entry requirements for a materials minor are effectively unclear.

missingTopics.topic: Structure-property relationships and materials science fundamentals
missingTopics.rationale: Only BME 304 Material Fundamentals touches this; a materials minor must center on the microstructure-to-property core (crystallography, defects, phase diagrams) that the current chemistry-heavy pool omits.
No characterization course appears, yet hands-on characterization is the defining laboratory skill of materials engineering.
The pool lacks any course on deformation, fracture and fatigue — essential for a minor that should let students reason about materials performance.

missingTopics.topic: Material classes: polymers, ceramics, composites and electronic materials
missingTopics.rationale: Beyond BME 304 and CHE 444, the minor has no coverage of specific engineering material families, the practical vocabulary of the field.

missingTopics.topic: A correctly scoped, materials-centered course pool
missingTopics.rationale: Because the listing is identical to the Chemistry Minor and chemistry-dominated, the most important fix is replacing it with genuine materials courses (starting from MTEN 201).

emergingTrends.rationale: Battery, fuel-cell and photovoltaic materials are among the fastest-growing materials-engineering areas; an energy-materials elective would modernize a minor currently built on general chemistry.
Data-driven materials discovery (ML for property prediction) is reshaping the field; building on the pool's physical-chemistry base could support such an elective.
3D-printed metals and polymers are transforming materials processing; a processing/AM component would give the minor contemporary relevance.
Circular-economy pressure is driving demand for recyclable and bio-based materials, a natural modern theme for a materials minor.
Semiconductors and functional materials underpin the electronics and energy sectors; explicit coverage would connect the minor to high-demand roles.

topSuggestions: Fix the data-extraction conflation and mislabeling: the pool is identical to the Chemistry Minor and chemistry-dominated — replace it with a genuine materials-engineering course set centered on MTEN 201 and BME 304.
Publish the materials minor's actual required/elective structure and total credits (all currently null).
Add the missing materials-engineering core (structure-property relationships, characterization, mechanical behavior, material classes) so the content matches the minor's title.
Publish 3-5 learning outcomes describing the materials competency the minor certifies.
Introduce a modern elective (energy/electronic materials or materials informatics) to give the minor current-practice relevance.
